articles.title: Editorial—President Hoover's Prohibition Pronouncement
articles.complete_text:
EDITORIAL
President Hoover's Prohibition Pronouncement

Throughout the land there is joy and rejoicing because of the prohibition pronouncement of President Hoover in his inaugural address, delivered March 4, 1929, in the city of Washington, D. C.

Carlton M. Sherwood, in an article, "Prohibition's First Real Chance," says: "At last prohibition has a vigorous and outstanding defender in the person of the President of the United States. Since its inception, prohibition has needed moral support from the White House. This it has not had in the past in any real sense. Prohibition has succeeded exceptionally well despite this lack, but with such support it would have succeeded immeasurably better.

"In the ten minutes following his inauguration, President Herbert Hoover said more in support of the great national policy of prohibiting the liquor traffic than has ever been said by any President at any time. He did not evade. He recognized the issue. He put it first in his inaugural. As though to give redoubled emphasis to the message to his countrymen, at the close of his inaugural oath, he leaned over the Bible and kissed the well known eighteenth verse of the twenty-ninth chapter of Proverbs—'Where there is no vision, the people perish, but he that keepeth the law, happy is he'.

"In our judgment, Herbert Hoover will do again and again what he did in his inaugural address in bringing the vital import-

EDITORIAL 253

ance of this question to the mind and heart of all the people. In his first message, President Hoover drove home the fact that 'it takes two to make a bootlegger', to use the searching phrase of Professor Horace D. Taft, brother of the Chief Justice of the United States. Already there is a new conscience abroad in the land on the matter. Many more or less neutral individuals, who were left cold by appeals of temperance bodies or lesser personages, have caught the implication and challenge of the President's inaugural appeal. He undoubtedly will need to speak several times, emphasizing one phase and another of this question. He begins by urging obedience because it is the law. If he continues, he will need to emphasize obedience not alone because it is the law, but because it is a good law. Both emphases are important."

It is a source of gratification to all people who really believe in prohibition to know that President Hoover has already indicated that he expects to have Congress transfer the prohibition enforcement bureau from the Treasury Department to the Department of Justice, under the Attorney-General. As it exists at present, the authority is divided. Placing prohibition in the Department of Justice, will give to it a unity that has been lacking in the present system. "No one can imagine," says Mr. Sherwood, "Herbert Hoover tolerating either corruption or inaction in the prohibition bureau or any other branch of the government."

But with all of Mr. Hoover's qualifications and desires, he cannot accomplish this great work alone. It behooves all government officials at Washington to stand squarely behind the President in this matter, and then, back of official Washington, should come the united support of all honorable men and women throughout the United States. It is our supreme chance to show to the world that the spiritual leadership we have assumed in this matter can be realized. We are in hearty accord with Mr. Sherwood when he says: "What, then, is the duty of those citizens who believe in prohibition? First, to trust and believe in President Hoover and give him a fair chance to start on this matter. Next, not to overwhelm him with advice or to criticise him too quickly on minor matters. This does not eliminate friendly criticism on vital matters, but it does preclude hasty and petulant criticism on trivial questions. He may not always do the wise thing, but it is our conviction he will always do what he believes to be the right thing. Let us 'have faith in Herbert Hoover'."
articles.notes: Found the complete editorial text in the PDF images. The article continues from page 248 to page 253 and discusses President Hoover's prohibition stance from his inaugural address.

articles.title: Utah Law Prohibits the Advertising of Tobacco and Cigarettes
articles.complete_text:
Utah Law Prohibits the Advertising of Tobacco and Cigarettes

Utah has a law which prohibits the advertising of tobacco and cigarettes. This law was passed by the legislature in 1921, and reads as follows:

"It shall be unlawful for any person, firm or corporation to advertise tobacco or cigarettes by means of billboards, signs, newspapers, magazines, or any other form of advertising within the state of Utah."

The penalty for violation of this law is a fine of not less than $25.00 nor more than $300.00, or imprisonment in the county jail for not more than six months, or both such fine and imprisonment.

This law was sponsored by the Relief Society and other organizations interested in the welfare of the youth of Utah. It has been quite effective in keeping tobacco advertising out of the state, although there have been some attempts to evade it.

The law does not prohibit the sale of tobacco products, but merely their advertising. This is considered a step in the right direction by those who are working for the elimination of the tobacco habit.
articles.notes: Article found on page 250 discussing Utah's 1921 law prohibiting tobacco and cigarette advertising

articles.title: Absent Camilla Woodbury Judd
articles.complete_text:
ABSENT

By Camilla Woodbury Judd

I miss you when the morning breaks
In glory o'er the hill,
And all the world around me wakes
But you are absent still.

I miss you when the noonday sun
Shines bright on field and lea,
And when the day's long work is done
You are not here with me.

I miss you when the evening star
Comes out to light the way
For those who journey from afar—
But you have gone to stay.

I miss you when the night winds sigh
Through lonely hours till dawn,
And most of all when morning's nigh
I know that you are gone.

But though you're absent from my sight,
And though we are apart,
You dwell within my soul's delight—
Forever in my heart.
articles.author: Camilla Woodbury Judd
articles.notes: Found complete poem on page 277 of the PDF images. It's a 5-stanza poem about missing someone who is absent.

articles.title: Notes from the Field
articles.complete_text:
NOTES FROM THE FIELD

The Relief Society of the Ogden Stake held a very successful conference on March 16th. The theme was "The Ideal Relief Society Sister." The speakers were Mrs. Amy Brown Lyman, general president; Mrs. Louise Y. Robison, general secretary; Mrs. Julia A. F. Child, general treasurer; and Mrs. Kate Montgomery Barker, member of the general board. The attendance was excellent and the spirit of the meeting was inspiring.

The Relief Society of the Weber Stake held their conference on March 23rd. The theme was "Relief Society Ideals." The speakers were Mrs. Amy Brown Lyman, general president; Mrs. Louise Y. Robison, general secretary; Mrs. Julia A. F. Child, general treasurer; and Mrs. Clarissa S. Williams, former general president. The attendance was large and the interest keen.

The Relief Society of the Davis Stake held their conference on March 30th. The theme was "The Relief Society Sister and Her Responsibilities." The speakers were Mrs. Amy Brown Lyman, general president; Mrs. Louise Y. Robison, general secretary; Mrs. Julia A. F. Child, general treasurer; and Mrs. Kate Montgomery Barker, member of the general board. The meeting was well attended and very successful.

The Relief Society of the Salt Lake Stake held their conference on April 6th. The theme was "Relief Society Objectives." The speakers were Mrs. Amy Brown Lyman, general president; Mrs. Louise Y. Robison, general secretary; Mrs. Julia A. F. Child, general treasurer; and Mrs. Clarissa S. Williams, former general president. The attendance was excellent and the spirit inspiring.

The Relief Society of the Granite Stake held their conference on April 13th. The theme was "The Relief Society Sister as a Citizen." The speakers were Mrs. Amy Brown Lyman, general president; Mrs. Louise Y. Robison, general secretary; Mrs. Julia A. F. Child, general treasurer; and Mrs. Kate Montgomery Barker, member of the general board. The meeting was well attended and very successful.
articles.notes: Found text on page 282 reporting on various Relief Society stake conferences. The text is somewhat difficult to read due to image quality, but the general content about stake conferences and speakers is clear.
